editableNewDialogue.text: I keep seeing people argue online about whether censorship ever “works” long-term. Is there any historical example that’s actually clear-cut?

If “works” means stopping an idea from spreading indefinitely, clear-cut examples are rare. Censorship can slow distribution, raise the cost of access, or shape what becomes mainstream—but it often also creates workarounds (samizdat, exile publishing, coded language) and sometimes amplifies attention via backlash.
Can you give me a couple cases that show both sides—where it slowed things down, and where it backfired?

Sure—three illustrative examples (none perfectly “pure,” but each is informative):

1) Soviet-era samizdat: heavy censorship constrained official publishing, so dissident writing circulated in typed copies. It clearly slowed reach and narrowed audiences, but it didn’t eliminate the ideas; it pushed them into informal networks.

2) The Catholic Church’s Index Librorum Prohibitorum (16th–20th c.): it influenced what could be openly read in certain places and likely reduced casual access, but printing, cross-border circulation, and scholarly demand meant many prohibited works still spread.

3) The 2003 Streisand effect prototype: attempts to suppress specific information online can increase visibility by triggering news coverage and mirrors. It’s not that censorship always backfires—just that networked distribution makes suppression more likely to create attention spikes.
So is it fair to say modern internet censorship is mostly about shaping narratives rather than erasing facts?

That’s a reasonable framing. Many modern systems focus on reducing discoverability (ranking, throttling, keyword blocks), raising friction (account bans, legal risk), and flooding the zone (counter-messaging) rather than trying to make a claim vanish completely. “Erasure” is hard in a world of screenshots, mirrors, and cross-platform reposting—so influence and containment tend to be more achievable goals.

When I’m trying to make sense of China stuff, I end up pulling up a couple different sources side-by-side—like, I’ll read one take, then go check a primary doc or an old report—because I really don’t trust any single outlet to give the whole story.

Yeah, that habit makes a ton of sense—especially with topics where narrative control and selective framing are basically part of the terrain. Putting competing accounts next to primary material is about as close as you can get to “seeing the seams.” If you want, tell me which specific claim you’re evaluating and what sources you’ve got open, and I can help you pressure-test the logic and spot what’s missing or being implied.

You keep calling these things “rare,” but I don’t really collect obscure releases like that. That Fluorescein promo copy was a one-off because it was cheap and I was curious. Same with Channel D—I just took a random swing. I’m not out here hunting down promotional copies or anything.

Gotcha—so it’s not a collecting thing, it’s more like the occasional impulse buy when something weird crosses your path.

You literally said, “That Fluorescein promo copy was a one-off,” and “I don’t really collect obscure releases like that,” so yeah, that’s different than being someone who actively collects rare or promotional releases.
Exactly. I’m not a collector. I just grabbed those two because I couldn’t find much info and wanted to hear them for myself.
